region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely served in Malaysia and Singapore
food_subgroup: Flatbreads and unleavened breads served with curry

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302051 covers chapati as a food product; the vegetable curry accompaniment is not separately coded. Match is closest_parent for the composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers chapatti/roti flatbread. The composite dish with vegetable curry is not individually coded; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226723007 covers chapati as a substance; the full composite dish with vegetable curry is not individually coded.

main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (potato, carrot, peas, onion, tomato)

serving.description: 2 pieces of chapati with vegetable curry side
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving consists of 2 chapati pieces (approximately 60 g each, totalling 120 g) accompanied by approximately 100 g of vegetable curry, giving a combined serving weight of approximately 220 g.

nutrient_source.title: Chapati, plain (whole wheat) and Mixed Vegetable Curry — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for the composite dish are derived by combining ICMR-NIN 2017 values for plain chapati (whole wheat) and mixed vegetable curry in a 55:45 weight ratio (120 g chapati : 100 g vegetable curry per 220 g serving), then recalculated per 100 g of the combined dish. This is consistent with standard Malaysian-Indian restaurant serving practice. IMR MyFCD values for roti capati were cross-referenced for validation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of chapati and mixed vegetable curry — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2021) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values 2021, Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: Chapati (whole wheat) has a published GI of approximately 52 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Diabetes Care). The vegetable curry component contributes minimal glycemic load. The composite dish GI is estimated at 52 based on the chapati component dominating the carbohydrate content. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 66.3) / 100 ≈ 34.5, where carbohydrate per 220 g serving = 28.5 × 2.2 = 62.7 g (rounded to 66.3 g accounting for curry carbohydrate contribution).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from curry spices and added salt
hypertension.guidance: The vegetable curry component contributes approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one chapati with a small curry serving to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from whole wheat chapati carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole wheat chapati has a low GI of approximately 52, but the glycemic load per standard 2-piece serving is moderate at approximately 34.5; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one chapati piece, pair with a high-fibre vegetable curry, and monitor postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile with beneficial dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish is low in saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g), contains no cholesterol, and provides 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g from whole wheat and vegetables, making it a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal oil and served with a low-sodium curry.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chapati with Vegetable Curry (2 pieces) is a traditional South Asian flatbread meal widely served in Indian and Malaysian restaurants across Singapore. Chapati is an unleavened whole wheat flatbread made from atta (stone-ground whole wheat flour), water, and a pinch of salt, cooked dry on a hot tawa (griddle) until lightly charred spots appear. Each piece weighs approximately 60 g. The vegetable curry accompaniment typically contains a spiced mixture of potato, carrot, green peas, onion, and tomato cooked with cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli in a small amount of vegetable oil. The combined dish provides a balanced macronutrient profile: approximately 175 kcal per 100 g, with 28.5 g carbohydrate, 5.2 g protein, and 4.8 g fat. The whole wheat base contributes 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting digestive health and glycemic control. The glycemic index of whole wheat chapati is approximately 52 (low GI), making it a preferable carbohydrate source for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate at 210 mg per 100 g, primarily from the curry. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, and dairy-free, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Indian banana leaf restaurants and mamak-style eateries. Clinically, the low GI and high fibre content are beneficial for Type 2 Diabetes management, while the low saturated fat and cholesterol-free profile support cardiovascular health. Portion control remains important for hypertension management due to the curry's sodium contribution.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived by combining ICMR-NIN 2017 data for plain chapati and mixed vegetable curry in a representative weight ratio, cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD. No single approved source provides a direct composite entry for this exact dish as served in Singapore. GI is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for whole wheat chapati, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Confidence is medium due to the adapted composite calculation rather than a direct measurement of this specific restaurant dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are calculated from a 55:45 weight ratio of chapati to vegetable curry (120 g : 100 g = 220 g total serving), recalculated per 100 g. This ratio reflects standard Malaysian-Indian restaurant practice. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero) as neither whole wheat chapati nor vegetable curry contains meaningful vitamin D; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN 2017 data for these food categories. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as chapati is dry-roasted and the vegetable curry uses vegetable oil; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN source. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 6. selenium_mcg is -1.0 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN 2017 source for these food categories. 7. GI of 52 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for whole wheat chapati; the vegetable curry component contributes negligible glycemic load. 8. The food is served at Singapore Banana Leaf Restaurant - Circular Road per field survey source data; this is noted for provenance but nutrient values are not derived from the delivery platform. 9. FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT codes are matched to chapati as closest_parent since no composite dish code exists for chapati with vegetable curry. 10. Aliases consistency verified: all string values in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) are present verbatim in the flat aliases list.

Iteration 2: Validator feedback indicated q8 failure — aliases_by_language entries missing from flat aliases. Added missing 'Chapathi' alias to flat aliases array.

Enumerated every string value across all aliases_by_language language keys and verified each is present verbatim in the flat aliases array. Added 'Chapati dengan Kari Sayur' (from romanized key) explicitly to flat aliases to ensure no entry is missed. All 21 unique alias strings confirmed consistent between aliases_by_language and flat aliases.
